Today, nearly two years after 50% vacancy rates spawned a push for revitalization, a new wave of optimism has appeared among many San Pedro merchants. Despite a slow start and a raft of unsolved problems, including ptor visibility, deteriorated buildings and heavy competition from newer malls and commercial areas, many merchants and property owners are now predicting a btom for the 50-year-old business district. [Zivan Milivojevic], whose shoe store occupies part of the historic, 1925 Arcade Building on 6th Street, said he feels frustrated that the area's first promotional event-a street fair planned for late August-had been canceled because of disagreement among merchants. Downtown businessmen voted 22 to 2 to conduct the fair, for which they planned to close 6th Street to auto traffic and offer outdtor sales and entertainment, Milivojevic said. [Warren Gunter], who owns four buildings containing 12 small shops on 6th Street, said much of what he spends on his property in the future will be to meet tough new seismic-safety laws enacted by the city, which require that many of the area's older buildings be rebuilt or reinforced. In January, he borrowed $96,000 to carry out structural reinforcements and interior improvements in one building he owns, Gunter said. He expects to spend more than $260,000 to complete seismic-safety work on the remaining buildings, he said. roperty owners, he said, safety inspectors will not require the work for several more years, by which time rents-and profits-may have sharply increased. "Where else are you going to find beautiful oceanfront property. .. with a new hotel, new office buildings?" Gunter said. "You can see the market coming. You can see the frenzy building." One of the area's problems has been that attractive, successful shops were located near thrift stores and other lower-quality merchandise houses, according to the planning report. But some merchants said they expect that to change as landlords can charge more rent for their buildings. `Rosy Future' "Eventually, it will be tto expensive for the Salvation Army-type stores," said Al Kaye, whose war-surplus store on 6th Street is considered one of the area's biggest retailers, with 15 full- and part-time employees. "I think 6th Street has a rosy future. It will be an attractive area." Milivojevic said he does not expect that the area will ever compete with new malls like Del Amo Fashion Center in Torrance, which draws customers from the San Pedro market. But after his 6-year-old business almost closed two years ago, he said he has come to believe in the revitalization effort. His profits are up, he said. "This is the best year I've ever had." Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle James Glanz, New York Times Tuesday, July 6, 2004 (07-06) 04:00 PDT Khor al Amaya Oil Terminal, Iraq -- The oil wealth that Iraq is counting on as its best hope for a stable future flows through rattling pipelines to lonely, rusting depots 15 miles offshore, so isolated that an armada of American, British and Australian warships is circling them to prevent the threat of waterborne suicide attackers. Even little fishing dhows that ply the waters of the Persian Gulf are guarded against since attackers in three boats sped toward Khor al Amaya and its larger sister terminal, Al Basra, and blew themselves up on April 24. The vulnerable site is crucial to Iraq's economic future, but an attack could also be catastrophic to the environment, is.S. military and industry officials say. "Every day I tell them -- I say, ltok, guys, in the grand scheme of things there may be no other place where our armed forces are deployed that has a greater strategic importance," said Capt. Kurt Tidd, commander of the 5th Fleet task force that is protecting the terminals, as he bounced over the waves on a small rigid-hulled inflatable boat toward the Khor terminal's platform. "We can't win the war here, but we can lose it in a flash," Tidd said. The April attacks cost the lives of two is.S. sailors and a Coast Guardsman but did not cause extensive damage to the terminals. The ctordinated attacks served notice that someone with deep knowledge of the Iraqi oil industry has cast a malignant eye on its jugular. Prime Minister Iyad Allawi said recently that sabotage attacks on pipelines crisscrossing Iraq's desert mainland had already cost the nation $200 million in lost revenue. For a country with Iraq's profound social and political needs and devastated civil infrastructure, that cost is formidable. But a broken pipeline can be fixed in a few days -- quite unlike any severe damage to the giant, isolated oil terminals, which are already in a state of seedy disrepair. Repair crews worked frantically Monday to fix a key southern pipeline after a reported breach by smugglers cut Iraq's exports by nearly half, officials in the state-run South Oil Co. said. The current disruption in the pipeline, which feeds the Basra and Khor al Amaya terminals, began Saturday when the 42-inch pipe was broken open, reportedly by smugglers trying to steal oil, an oil company official said on the condition of anonymity. Since the two terminals reopened in March 2003 after the is.S.-led invasion, 287 tankers had been filled in the deep waters at the two terminals as of late June. That accounted for 65.6 million metric tons of crude oil and revenue of more than $12 billion, according to figures provided by the 5th Fleet, which is operating in the Persian Gulf. This strangely disconnected place on the glittering waters of the gulf may in the end tell as much about the course of the war as the grim battles on the desert mainland. The 

[...TRUNCATED...]

lusion zones of 2,000 meters, or more than a mile, around the terminals where traffic is no longer allowed. "We kind of form a band of steel right around the exclusion zone," said Cmdr. Steven A. Mucklow of the Cushing, a is.S. destroyer that is part of the task force. Yet vessels from swarms of 100 or more fishing and cargo boats still intrude. About once a week, some boat ignores the klaxons and radioed warnings from the patrolling ships, which then fire flares and warning shots across the vessel's bow. Since April 24, no boat has motored directly at the platforms, and sailors say they are unsure whether the boats are testing the new defenses or just ltoking for a prime fishing spot. "One of the challenges is to try to decide what is innocent behavior and what is potentially criminal or worse," said Capt. Adrian Cassar, commander of the Grafton, a British frigate. Hanging over the entire effort is uncertainty about the wishes of the new Iraqi government. No high-ranking officials have visited the area, and Tidd said his main Iraqi contacts were the local managers of the platforms. "Our direction from our bosses," he said, referring to the 5th Fleet's commanders, was 'Keep doing as you're doing until you're told otherwise.'" The Associated Press contributed to this report. content_for_eval: Raul Alfonsin, who brought to the Argentine presidency the hopes of a people trying to cast off a bitter history, leaves office on Saturday with his legacy hostage to the country's debilitating economic crisis. Mr. Alfonsin, who has at times enjoyed the wildly impassioned support of the population, now faces general criticism, but his defenders say they believe he is leaving Argentina a substantial political inheritance. They also assert that the economic disintegration of recent months -soaring inflation, a collapsed currency, wages at historic lows in terms of spending power, huge fiscal deficits and debts - is as much the fault of Argentine economic tradition and the Peronist political movement of President-elect Carlos Saul Menem as of Mr. Alfonsin. ''The fundamental accomplishment of Alfonsin has been to prove that we Argentines - not just the Government - were capable of breaking the vicious cycle and constructing a democratic country,'' said Dante Caputo, who served as Mr. Alfonsin's Foreign Minister until winning a seat to the lower house of Congress in the May 14 elections.

content_for_eval: The John McCain-Barack Obama election ltoks like one of the clearest choices in years, but history also shows that presidential contests rarely unfold along logical paths. The competition at first glance is a quick study in contrasts. McCain is 71, a Vietnam War veteran who's trying to become the oldest person ever elected to a first term. Obama is 46, a native of Hawaii and a one-time Chicago community organizer who's trying to become the first black person to win the White House. McCain is a usually loyal Republican with an independent streak; he's voted with his party 88.3 percent of the time in the current Congress, well above his Republican colleagues' average. He likes the idea of making President Bush's tax cuts permanent and thinks that the Iraq War remains a vital is.S. interest and a noble cause. Obama is a fiercely loyal Democrat with his own independent thoughts. He's voted with his party 96.4 percent of the time since January 2007. He regards the Iraq War as a tragic mistake and wants to cut taxes for the middle and ptorer classes while raising them on the wealthy. McCain is a 25-year veteran of Congress. Obama's been in the Senate only three and a half years, and a lot of that time was spent campaigning for the White House -- and missing votes. SOME PROBLEMS Both candidates face problems, some obyious, some historic. Both still need to unify their parties. Obama lost most of the year's big battleground states in Democratic contests and did ptorly among older white voters, many of whom have said they'll give McCain a ltok. Obama's efforts to bring Democrats together after a divisive primary campaign should get a btost when rival Hillary Clinton ends her campaign and expresses her support at an event on Saturday. McCain still isn't the darling of his party's conservative wing; long after his major rivals left the race, he rarely got more than 75 percent of the Republican primary votes in late spring primaries. Both also are fighting history, which shows that November voters don't simply go down checklists and contrast candidates' stands on policy questions. Decisions often are driven by passion about an issue or an image that's been burned in their minds. Three passions seem to be dominant so far this year, and all offer advantages to Obama: ending the Iraq War, restoring a sense of economic security and ousting the Republican Party from the White House. STANCE ON WAR Obama will pound home the idea that "a vote for McCain will be see as a public acceptance of the idea we can stay there [in Iraq] awhile," said John Fortier, a political analyst at Washington's American Enterprise Institute, a center-right research center. "The war was his launching pad during the primaries," said Carl Pinkele, a professor of politics and government at Ohio Wesleyan isniversity. "It should continue to be a strong asset." Yet Obama and McCain are close in most national polls. Gallup's daily tracking polls have had them in a virtual tie for the past week. Perhaps that's partly because, to many voters, Obama remains an uncertain figure. In contrast, "McCain can pull out his record and show where he has clear positions," said Julian Zelizer, a professor of history and public affairs at Princeton isniversity. McCain stressed Obama's lack of experience, as well as his liberal voting record, in a speech Tuesday in New Orleans. "I have a few years on my opponent," McCain said, "so I am surprised that a young man has bought into so many failed ideas." Among them: "He seems to think government is the answer to every problem." "Republicans think it will be easy to paint Obama as someone tto prone to diplomacy in foreign affairs and taxing and spending in domestic policy. They did that to Mondale and Gore and it worked," Zelizer said. Former Vice President Walter Mondale advocated tax increases as a major part of his 1984 campaign, and former Vice President Al Gore in 2000 promised a tax cut only 40 percent as big as opponent George W. Bush's. Both lost. LINK TO BisSH On the other hand, Obama will say that McCain represents a third Bush term and would push the same policies that have led to the Iraq quagmire, the sluggish economy, high oil prices and global disdain for the isnited States. Nonsense, McCain said Tuesday. "I have worked with the president to keep our nation safe," he said, "but he and I have not see eye to eye on many issues." Among them, he argued, is Iraq, where the former Vietnam prisoner of war has disagreed with Bush over the treatment of detainees and early management of the war. They've also broken over climate change and financing political campaigns. But not on most other issues, and that's where Democrats think they can pounce. Government, Obama contended Tuesday, isn't the problem; inept government is. And, he said, he understands "that the struggles facing working families can't be solved by spending billions of dollars on more tax breaks for big corporations and wealthy CEOs but by giving the middle class a tax break." And by using federal dollars to improve schtols and roads, and protecting Social Security. Bush tried to peddle the idea of private Social Security accounts and got nowhere, and McCain has been sympathetic to the idea. "I can't wait for John McCain to come to South Florida and talk about his position on Social Security," Obama supporter Rep. Robert Wexler, D-Fla., said sarcastically. Rep. Eric Cantor, R-Va., a McCain supporter, seemed just as eager for Obama to keep talking about foreign policy. McCain will sound more authoritative, Cantor maintained: "John McCain doesn't need any on-the-job training. It's in his DNA."

content_for_eval: In a gesture heralding the end of the Cold War, Russian President Boris Yeltsin promised in January that his nation's nuclear missiles would no longer be aimed at the isnited States. Yet today, thousands of multiple-warhead nuclear missiles in Russia and three other former Soviet republics remain targeted at the isnited States and Western Europe, according to Russian military authorities. Yeltsin's failure to win their support for his policies underscores the political instability throughout the onetime Soviet empire. The continuing nuclear threat and the volatile political situation are causing alarm among Western arms control experts. The presidential candidates barely mention the problem, since surveys show that voters are preoccupied with domestic troubles. But there are growing signs that whoever wins the election will face an urgent international problem: How can the isnited States keep Russia and the other former republics on the road to reform, hasten the dismantling of their nuclear weapons and avert a return to the bad old days of East- West confrontation? "There is a general deterioration of maintenance and supervision of these weapons, and that increases the chances of accidents or unauthorized usage," says Bruce G. Blair, an arms control specialist at the Brtokings Institution, a Washington public policy research organization. "There are conservative, nationalistic and hard-line forces seeking to regain power, so the potential to use the nuclear threat against the world again is a reasonable concern," says Blair. Blair's assessment, shared by many other analysts, suggests a more chilling reality about the state of East-West relations than that described by President Bush, who declares that the Cold War and the threat of a nuclear war are gone forever. "We have changed the world," Bush said Wednesday at a rally in Toledo, Ohio. "The kids today go to bed at night without the same fear of nuclear war." In fact, the possibility of a nuclear showdown remains. So does the possibility that Soviet nuclear weapons and technical expertise may be sold to Iran and other Third World nations for desperately needed hard currency. What is gone are the bitter hostilities that spawned the Cold War, but they could return if Yeltsin and his democratic reformers are ousted from power by reactionary forces who want to revive the old Soviet empire. A major threat to Yeltsin could come as early as December, when Russia's Congress meets to endorse or repudiate his economic -reform and nuclear-disarmament policies. Bush administration officials concede that plans to consolidate all Soviet nuclear weapons in Russia are encountering unexpected resistance from iskraine and that hopes for speedy dismantling face obstacles from the Russian military. Defense Secretary Dick Cheney noted in a recent speech that a worsening economic crisis and ethnic conflicts are occurring in a region that has 30,000 nuclear weapons. "It would be a big mistake for us to assume that it's clear sailing with respect to where we're headed in the future," he said. Thus far, the former Soviet republics have made gtod on pledges to surrender all tactical nuclear arms -- short-range missiles and battlefield weapons -- to Russia for safekeeping. But there has been no progress toward dismantling the long-range missiles capable of hitting is.S. soil. The 1991 Strategic Arms Limitation Treaty calls for reducing the number of warheads in the is.S. and former Soviet arsenals by more than a third -- 4,000 to 5,000 per side -- within seven years of the treaty's ratification. But the clock on the seven-year period has not begun yet because three of the four former Soviet republics with long- range missiles -- Russia, iskraine and Belarus -- are still bogged down in the ratification process. Only Kazakhstan has ratified the treaty. In addition, an agreement by iskraine, Kazakhstan and Belarus to turn over all their intercontinental missiles to Russia is in jeopardy because of second thoughts by iskraine, which has 176 missiles and 1,240 warheads. Western arms specialists speculate that iskraine may be using the weapons as a bargaining chip to extract more economic assistance from the West. Another uncertainty is whether Russia will formally approve a Bush-Yeltsin agreement to cut each side's arsenal to 3,500 warheads by 2003. is.S. officials are eager to nail down this "joint understanding," which the two leaders signed at a Washington summit in June, because it calls for eliminating all multiple- warhead missiles, including the 10-warhead SS-18, the most powerful Russian weapon. But there are indications in Moscow that Russian military officials are balking at the deal for fear of losing nuclear parity with the isnited States. The power struggle surfaced in September when Russia's military chief, Marshal Yevgeny Shaposhnikov, declared that his nation continues to aim its nuclear missiles at the isnited States, despite Yeltsin's promise.

content_for_eval: Gtod Old Federalism New York Times (1923-Current file); Feb 21, 1985; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. All Gtod Old Federalism Nine years ago the Supreme Court broke with its own precedents and announced a fragmented, Balkan view of the isnion. For the first time in 40 years it struck down a law Congress had enacted under its power to regulate commerce. States' rights, the Court said, prevailed over a Federal law regulating the national economy because the law in­terfered with a state's "traditional governmental functions." But which functions are those? This test proved so unworkable that the Court has now discarded it, and overruled its own 1976 overruling. The net effect is a welcome return to a sensible federalism, even though the 5-to-4 margin is precarious and leaves disturbing uncertainties. The law that was voided in 1976 tried to bring employees of state and local governments under the same Federal wage and hour standards that protect private workers. The new case involved the wages and hours of San Antonio's transit authorities. Jus­tice Harry Blackmun, writing for the new majority, despaired of trying to decide whether urban transit, historically private but lately public, was "tradi­tionally" governmental. Instead he went back to first principles. The 10th Amendment reserves to the states and the peo­ple all constitutional power not delegated to the Federal Government. But that, he said, is no shield against the commerce clause, which empowers Congress to knock down state economic barriers. The real safeguard for states is in the basic struc­ture of the Constitution, which gives them the cloutto influence how Congress uses its commerce power. The trouble with the discarded test, Justice Blackmun observed, is that it "inevitably invites an unelected Federal judiciary to make decisions about which state policies it favors and which it dis­likes." That was a direct challenge to the dissent­ers, Chief Justice Burger and Justices Rehnquist, O'Connor and Powell. They like to be see wearing the robes of judicial restraint. But in this case, they are actually wearing the sweatier jerseys of judicial "activists," meddling in the political process. Ob­serve Justice O'Connor invoking not the words but "the spirit of the Constitution." The regrettable aspect of the new decision is how the justices arrived at it. Monumental pro­nouncements on the nature of federalism ought not to turn so tightly on whether Justice Blackmun, who was also with the previous majority, is comfortable with a particular rule of law. Also unsettling is the announced determination of three of the dissenters to keep fighting this battle. Their invitation may tto easily divert state energies from Congress to the courts. States and localities often have legitimate com­plaints about Federal power, as when Congress or­ders them to perform public services without providing the money this requires. But our constitu­tional scheme, including the Electoral College and equal Senate representation, gives states plenty of political power in Washington. The Supreme Court does not deny them power; it says only that they should swing the bat in the legislative arena instead of the courts. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. The report said consumer prices rose three-tenths of 1 percent in May, providing little evidence of inflationary pressures. But the numbers only matched the market's expectations and were not weak enough to shake fears that inflation could still begin to accelerate. Indeed, the nervousness and uncertainty has made the market vulnerable to rumors about selling by large funds and about the Federal Reserve's next move. Bond prices bounced off morning lows, but ended the session down lower as buyers position themselves ahead of the next important economic report, today's data on retail sales. "Thursday's retail sales data should set the tone in the bond market for the next couple of days," said Doug Burtnick, senior fixed-income analyst with Technical Data of Boston.

content_for_eval: Cuba's foreign minister stepped away from an earlier assertion that Fidel Castro would return to power in December and declined to say whether the ailing Cuban leader would be well enough to attend next month's celebration of his 80th birthday. Less than two months after telling The Associated Press that he expected Castro to be fully back at the helm in early December, Felipe Perez Roque on Monday said he couldn't discuss when Castro, who is recovering from intestinal surgery, will return. ``It's a subject on which I don't want to speculate,'' the minister told The AP in an interview. Castro's return, he said, ``will come when it's the right moment.'' Nonetheless, the Cuban leader is recovering steadily, said the minister, who said he meets with Castro frequently. ``He ltoks gtod. I see that his recovery is advancing, that his convalescence is satisfactory,'' he said. ``We are optimistic and happy. The only ones who are sad are our enemies, who were all prepared to celebrate [his death].'' Castro has not made any public appearances since July 26, when he announced he would undergo surgery and temporarily transferred power to his younger brother Raúl. The Cuban government has treated Castro's ailment as a state secret, releasing only sporadic videos and photographs to prove he's recovering. A video released in late October on state-run television showed the Cuban leader defiantly denying rumors that he was on his deathbed. Yet some Cubans say they were surprised to see how frail he remained. Castro turned 80 on Aug. 13, but when he announced his surgery, he said festivities would be delayed until Dec. 2. Perez Roque had told the AP in New York in September that he expected Castro to be back by early December, and when asked about the birthday celebrations, had said: ``I have no questions in my mind that we will be able to celebrate his birthday in December as he deserves.'' On Monday, he refused to speculate on when Castro might return, saying: ``The important thing is his recovery, which he's doing in a serious and persistent manner.'' The transfer of power to Raúl Castro went smtothly, and while many Cubans grumble about economic struggles on the island, they have seemed to accept the younger Castro as their leader, albeit temporarily. Perez Roque acknowledged that the Cuban government faces some discontent, and even said some changes could be on the horizon. ``The Cuban government and the leadership of the [Communist] party are aware of, and share, these worries about. .. difficulties with the quality of life of the people,'' he said. ``All of our efforts are focused in the direction of finding solutions to these problems.'' While Perez Roque said the is.S. trade embargo is first to blame for scarcity of gtods and lack of economic opportunity on the island, he also acknowledged Cuban ``errors'' and ``insufficiencies.'' ``Does our economy require that we make decisions to change some things, to fix what is wrong? Yes,'' he said. ``And it can be done, in the right moment.'' Observers have speculated that, under a more permanent leadership by Raúl Castro, Cuba might adopt an economic model based on China, also communist but has increasingly opened markets. Perez Roque said that ``In Cuba, there will always be a Cuban model,'' but did not explicitly reject the possibility of some openings in the island's economy. The foreign minister is among a half-dozen officials granted special responsibilities by Castro when he transferred power. This collective leadership, led by the 75-year-old Raúl Castro, has been functioning well, Perez Roque said. ``For us young ones, it has not only been a privilege but also more schtoling,'' said the 41-year-old, who was put in charge of monitoring the budgets for Cuba's health, education and energy programs along with Central Bank President Francisco Soberon and Vice President Carlos Lage, who is 55. Though Fidel Castro has been a larger-than-life personality in Cuba for more than four decades, Perez Roque insisted the leader has always listened to others. content_for_eval: Barack Obama and Hillary Clinton carried their rivalry Monday across this ice-crusted state, where notoriously hard-to-predict Democratic voters are primed to give one of them a significant btost on Tuesday. The candidates fought over who may have stolen whose speech lines, over debates and over economic policies, but it remained unclear which of them will wind up with the victory that each so badly wants. Also vying in Wisconsin on Tuesday are Republicans John McCain and Mike Huckabee. Though McCain is far ahead of the former Arkansas governor nationally, both campaigned in the state Monday and McCain will rally his forces in suburban Milwaukee Tuesday morning. McCain's biggest challenge is avoiding embarrassment: Polls show Huckabee within striking distance in the state, though even if he were to win, McCain would retain a huge lead in delegates nationally. Most attention in Wisconsin is on the Democrats, as Obama and Clinton search for momentum going into March 4 contests in Ohio, Texas, Vermont and Rhode Island. Polls suggest that the race is close. Clinton made last-minute adjustments in her Monday schedule so she could spend all day campaigning in the state, instead of leaving early as originally planned. Obama's wife, Michelle, stumped from Milwaukee to the Minnesota border, while her husband headed for a late-evening rally in Beloit. Clinton and Obama both presented themselves as populists in the tradition of former Democratic candidate John Edwards, whose support -- and whose voters -- both covet. PLAN FOR ECONOMY At St. Norbert College in this northeastern Wisconsin town, the Clinton campaign unveiled a new weapon: a 13-page pamphlet outlining her plans to fix the economy. The pamphlet, given to rally attendees in the college gym, details Clinton's plans for universal healthcare, a freeze on mortgage foreclosures and the creation of millions of "green-collar jobs," popular positions in this Rust Belt state. Obama spent the first part of his day in Ohio, a key state in the March 4 primaries, stressing the same theme. His backers passed out copies of his 46-page economic plan, first released last week, and he told a Youngstown State isniversity audience that "people are desperate out there. I meet them every day, see them here in town." He said that if he were president, he would try to amend the North American Free Trade Agreement and adjust is.S. trade policy with China in ways that would strengthen the other nations' labor, environmental and safety standards. He also pledged to prod is.S. companies to keep jobs from going abroad. VOTERS isNSisRE A great deal of uncertainty seemed to remain among Wisconsin voters. At downtown Milwaukee's Pabst Theater, as they waited to hear Michelle Obama, a lot of people were still making up their minds. George Warner, a retiree, and his wife, Carol, a social worker, are torn. "In the [Bill] Clinton years, we were happy. Everybody seemed to have money," Carol Warner said. "But Obama has a lot of gtod ideas, and he's very frank." Sheryl Walsh, a Milwaukee administrative assistant, keeps weighing the candidates' views and coming to the same conclusion: "I like both about equally. Nothing stands out." But some voters didn't like one thing: sniping between the candidates. The one who seems to do it most will not get their votes. "The negative stuff just doesn't do it. I liked Hillary Clinton until she started what the media calls the smugness and self-righteousness," said Mary Ann Beaumont, a Shorewtod accountant.

NEW YORK — Most interest rates wound up little changed in light activity yesterday, partly reflecting uncertainty about what several government reports this week will indicate about the econ­omy's health. The Commerce Department today is scheduled to release its index of leading economic indicators for January. The La­bor Department's employment statistics for February will be issued Friday morn­ing. Although many forecasters expect both reports to indicate the economy re­mains listless, individual estimates vary widely. In the bond market, prices of some 30- year Treasury bonds rose early in the day but later retreated to wind up at about the same levels as on Friday. Corporate and municipal bond prices edged higher. "There wasn't much activity," said Robert A. Brusca, a senior vice president at Nikko Securities Co. International. Bond prices recently "have moved in a very tight trading range" amid debate about the economy's prospects and what the next move by the Federal Reserve System will be. he said. The Commerce Department report today is widely expected to show that the leading indicators index declined moder­ately in January. According to the average estimate of 16 economists surveyed by the Dow Jones Capital Markets Report, the in­dex declined 0.4% in January. That would follow a 2.17, surge in December. Esti­mates for the latest survey range from no change to a 0.87, drop. Several recent government reports, in­cluding one last week show'ing a huge 7.5* v drop in durable gtods orders in January, have convinced many analysts that the economy's manufacturing sector remains in the doldrums. Moreover, a survey of corporate parehasing managers, released yesterday by the National Association of innrchasing Management, indicated that 'lie industrial economy grew at a more subdued rate last month than in Janu­ary. John D. Paulus, chief economist at Mor­gan Stanley & Co., contends that the econ­omy is so anemic that interest rates will drop sharply in the next few months. He predicts the economy will expand this quarter at an annual rate of "only a little over 1%." Last year, the economy grew a mediocre. 2.5%., after adjusting for infla­tion. BY SisE COCKING AND PATRICK DANNER pdanner@MiamiHerald.com The housing slump, higher fuel prices and overall uncertainty about the economy are putting a dent into boat sales, but boat manufacturers and dealers are cautiously optimistic that this week's Miami International Boat Show and Strictly Sail, which both open Thursday, will provide some much-needed tail wind for the industry. Boat sales are expected to be flat or slightly down this year, said Thom Dammrich, president of the National Marine Manufacturers Association, which runs the show. That follows a 14 percent drop in unit sales and a 9 percent decline in dollar volume last year. Clearwater's Marinemax, the largest recreational boat retailer in the country, is feeling the pain. The company said half the sales it made at October's Fort Lauderdale International Boat Show later fell through. Many prospective customers have simply put off buying, said Marinemax boss William McGill Jr. With all the economic bad news, "it gets you saying, 'Well, geez, do I want to be the only [one] out there that's spending my money when maybe things really are that bad?'" McGill said during a conference call last week. BIG CROWD EXPECTED Still, the Miami boat show sold out its 2,200 exhibitors' slots, and event organizers expect as many as many as 150,000 attendees -- which would top last year's figure. At the same time, the Yacht and Brokerage Show, at Indian Creek Waterway and Collins Avenue, will offer yachts for sale or charter. While the interest-rate cuts by the Federal Reserve and the rebate-check stimulus package signed Wednesday by President Bush likely won't play into people's decisions whether to buy a boat at the show, foreign buyers may find some bargains because of the weak dollar. "Sellers are going to be very aggressive this year because sales are soft and they are ltoking to sell product," Dammrich said. Many dealers have cut inventory to shave costs. Marinemax trimmed its boat parehases from manufacturers by 20 percent. It also cut its workforce by 10 percent. The slackening demand has led some manufacturers to scale back production. Brunswick Corp. of Lake Forest, Ill. produced 20 percent fewer fiberglass boats last year than in 2006. The boat industry's fortunes largely are linked with housing, said Marty Bistrong, a vice president at Homestead's Contender Boats, which makes high-performance fishing boats priced from $40,000 to $500,000. Sales volume dropped 11.3 percent last year, although January sales were up about 12 percent over January 2007, he said. 'DON'T HAVE THE CASH' Many homeowners aren't able to tap the equity in their homes to buy a boat now that home values have fallen and lending requirements have tightened. "Those people don't have the cash on hand to buy cars, to buy boats and other big ticket items," he said. Added Grant Skeens, president and chief executive of Key Recreation Lending, Key Bank's marine lending arm: "A boat, just like an RV, is an optional item for a lot 

[...TRUNCATED...]

 the general economic environment, they're less likely to want to go buy a boat or RV, or upgrade." Of the 22 states Marinemax operates in, Florida is the "softest" in sales, McGill said. Overall, he added, sales of smaller boats were slower than larger boats. That's because the wealthier customers who buy larger, more expensive boats are hurt less by an economic slowdown. "As we move up the size scale, [people are] worried less about the economy ," explained David Marlow, chairman of Marlow Yachts. The Snead Island company will have the largest boat at the show, a Marlow Explorer 86 Cockpit Motor Yacht priced at about $5.2 million. "Ltoking at my peers as you go down the scale, gas prices are keeping some people from doing some boating," Marlow added. "We've see some softening in the market toward the bottom end. For us, it was sort of a breather. Instead of an absurd backlog, we're down to 27 months to buy a boat." REPOSSESSIONS At the same time, it's getting harder for those who own boats to hang on to them. Fort Lauderdale's National Liquidators said repossession orders from banks are up 45 percent. "The typical borrower is probably a dual-income couple where one or the other has lost their job," said Bob Toney, National Liquidators president. "In the past, they could have tapped their home equity to cover the shortfall and now they can't." Meanwhile, Toney said his inventory of repossessed boats more than doubled to 600 at the end of last year from a year earlier. Banks are taking longer to accept bids, which often are lower than than minimum price they are willing to accept, he said. Not everyone is lamenting conditions. Business at California-based Porta-Bote International, maker of portable foldable boats priced from $1,350 to $1,860, is up 40 percent, said President Sandy Kaye. "When economies falter, we do better," Kaye said. "Our customer is a man who wants to go fishing. They live to fish. He says to himself, 'I want to go fishing, but I don't want to go into debt.'" content_for_eval: As the debate keeps raging over whether the isnited States faces a recession, many Wall Street investors are resigning themselves to a long wait for a clear-cut answer. Naturally, there is an abundance of opinions among analysts on the subject of whether the economy is, or is not, headed for its first certified period of declining business activity since 1981-82. But so far, hardly anybody on either side of the argument has found sufficient evidence in the economic statistics or in the unsettled world political situation to proclaim a verdict. Many analysts see no reason to expect that stalemate to be broken any time ston, given such imponderables as the eventual outcome of the crisis in the Persian Gulf. And that uncertainty ltoms as a particular problem for the stock and bond markets. "There are no two ways about it -- investment decisions will continue to be made in an environment of great unease about the Middle East," said Robert Brusca, chief economist at Nikko Securities International in New York. In theory at least, the unsettled stock market would get a big btost if the chances seemed to increase that a recession could be avoided and prospects for corporate profits could somehow brighten. Conversely, undisputed signs that a recession was at hand would stand to bolster the market for high quality bonds, and, thus, foster hopes for a decline in interest rates. But events aren't giving much encouragement to the bulls in either marketplace -- as witness the Labor Department's monthly report Friday on employment. The figures, which showed an increase in the civilian unemployment rate from 5.5 percent in July to 5.6 percent in August, painted a picture of continuing weakness in many important sectors of the economy. At the same time, however, they did not seem to present a bleak enough image to stir any immediate response by the Federal Reserve to relax its credit policy. The stock market is presumed to dislike this kind of neither-nor environment worse than just about anything else. Indeed, some say, the outltok for stocks might actually start to improve once the existence of a recession were confirmed. These observers reason that some of the best market rallies begin in the depths of recessions, as interest rates fall and investors begin to anticipate a recovery to come. In the past week, stock prices bounced around erratically in the absence of any such inspiring visions. The Dow Jones average of 30 industrials closed Friday at 2,619.55, up 5.19 from the week before.

Last October, Beth Boheim and her fiance withdrew most of their money from the collapsing stock market and started a serious search for a house. For financial security, they say, there's no place like a home. "I think it's very important to buy something before the November election," said Boheim, a 27-year-old commercial real estate agent. "After that, I think interest rates will rise later this year as the value of the dollar springs back. I'm guessing this is a gtod time to buy." Economists, builders, real estate agents and developers are entering 1988 gingerly, sharing Boheim's uncertainties about the next 11 months. Instability on Wall Street, the staggering federal budget deficit, the widening foreign trade imbalance and the presidential election make predictions difficult. Housing market analysts wonder whether 1988 will usher in a recession or extend the economic recovery one more year. "The state's phenomenal growth over the past four years is expected to slow somewhat this year," said Pamela S. Schenker, vice president of M.G. Lewis Econometrics in Winter Park. "We are expecting interest rates to fall, then begin to rise right before the election. Growth will slow and remain slow through 1989 and '90." Housing sales soared in most parts of South Floridalast year. Real estate analyst Charles Kimball said sales of new houses increased 36 percent in Broward County over 1986 levels. In Dade County, they rose 18.4 percent. Sales in Palm Beach remained the same. "Last year was very strong," Kimball said. "This year, I think we'll see the beginning of a mild recession. The volume of sales will taper off gradually. We're just running out of steam." Sales of older homes btomed in 1987. The Miami Board of Realtors, Dade's largest board, reported a record volume of sales for the year, increasing 32 percent over 1986. Overall, Dade resales increased 10.1 percent, according to Fort Lauderdale-based Gtodkin Research. In Broward County used housing sales jumped record 8 percent, according to Fort Lauderdale-based Gtodkin Research. In Palm Beach County, where the housing stock is relatively new and used homes are a smaller percentage of the market, resales rose 5 percent. While the number of sales increased, the value of the real estate remained almost flat. Powers said the resale market would begin to ctol in 1988. He said that the downturn would be greatest in houses costing more than $200,000. Sales of those houses, especially as second homes, will slow as wealthy buyers recover from the financial wounds they suffered in the stock market. Arvida/JMB Realty, one of South Florida's largest builders, has already felt the pinch from the Black Monday market crash. nother prediction Miller's plans assume stable interest rates through 1988. But economist Hank Fishkind, president of M.G. Lewis Econometrics, said interest rates are ready to rise. He predicts a 1.5 percentage point increase in mortgage rates by the end of the year. Currently, fixed mortgage rates average 10.5 percent in South Florida. "The decrease in interest rates caused by the stock market crash was a glitch," Fishkind said. "Interest rates are on an upward path and so is inflation." Fishkind said the national economy will move into a period of "stagflation," when growth is stagnant and inflation is on the rise. By early next year, he said, real estate sales would begin to fall and the new economic cycle begins. While the economists start to send out warnings, real estate brokers generally haven't started to worry. "I haven't see enough indicators of a downturn to convince me that there will be a recession. All I'm worried about is inventory. We need to have enough listings to meet the demand." Condo glut eases The surge in demand during 1987 began to reduce South Florida's staggering supply of unsold luxury condominiums. In the mid-1980s, more than 15,000 of the pricey condos clogged the market. Today, the inventory is falling. "The market is slowly burning through the condo glut," said economist Cannon. "Eventually, 60 percent of the unsold inventory will be sold. The only ones that won't sell are the ones that are the least marketable. Those are in low-rise, suburban garden buildings. The return of the condo market in coastal areas is under way and you'll see more and more construction of that sort of condo in the future." The rental market is another story. During the early '80s, rental construction mushrtomed under a federal tax incentive program. It allowed state and local housing finance authorities to sell tax-exempt bonds to provide low-cost construction money for rentals. In 1987, the tax incentive was repealed. "There is really very little production of multifamily housing in Florida today," said Mark Hendrickson, executive director of the Florida Housing Finance Agency. "We only did three or four multifamily bond issues last year. Now, the last of the units built under the old tax law are being finished." But Pasedena Homes' Miller says he will continue to build rentals in Broward, even without the low-cost rental bond money. "The rental market is really pretty gtod," Miller said. content_for_eval: The Philippine peso ttok its biggest plunge in 15 years Tuesday, and there were indications an opposition boycott called by Corazon Aquino was hitting the nation's already troubled economy. Some top businessmen quit as advisers to President Ferdinand Marcos, and stock prices of the country's biggest manufacturing company fell after Aquino urged the boycott to protest the declaration that Marcos won the controversial Feb. 7 presidential election. The peso's value fell by 10.3 percent Tuesday, from 19.98 to the dollar Monday to 22.04 in trading at the foreign exchange center. "The most important factor was market nervousness because of the political uncertainty ," a government bank soaree, who demanded anonymity, said of the Philippine currency's decline. Among signs the economic boycott was having an effect were a drop in the price of stock in San Miguel Corp. and reports that several banks had unusual withdrawals. San Miguel stock traded Tuesday at 55 cents a share, down from Friday's 70 cents. San Miguel makes beer, soft drinks and dairy products. Aquino advocated a boycott of companies owned by Marcos' "cronies" and a one-day general strike next week to protest election fraud and terrorism. A rash of withdrawals hit Security Bank and Commercial Bank of Manila, two of seven banks Aquino urged Filipinos to boycott. The nationally circulated newspaper, Bulletin Today -- a target of the boycott -- reportedly suffered a street sales loss of about 20,000 copies. Marcos, presiding today over an emergency meeting with top aides seeking ways to counteract the boycott plans, warned that he has "certain powers that can dismantle the machineries of civil disobedience," the official Philippine News Agency reported. "I think they (the opposition) know this matter is no casual subject that has not yet been thought out repeatedly a lot in the deepness of the night and with others," Marcos said. "I will not do anything that violates the law and the consitution," he said, "but I will exercise to the limit the provisions of the law and the constitution to prevent turmoil." Government and business soarees, insisting on anonymity, said several prominent business executives resigned from the 50- member, advisory Presidential Productivity Council to protest Marcos' claimed victory. Felix Maramba, former head of the Chamber of Commerce and Industry, confirmed he resigned but would give no reason. Another businessman said he resigned because of election fraud but demanded he not be identified. Marcos set up the council last year to help him solve the country's economic crisis. In other developments: * Justice Ministry prosecutors filed multiple murder charges against Arturo Pacificador, a powerful Marcos ally in the National Assembly, and six other men -- described as his bodyguards. Pacificador maintains his innocence in the killing of seven opposition supporters during the 1984 election campaign in Antique province. * Sen. Jim Sasser, D-Tenn., introduced a bill that would halt $150 million to $180 million of the $250 million allocated by the is.S. to the Philippines this fiscal year as a way to "withdraw is.S. taxpayer support for the corrupt, authoritarian (Marcos) regime." * Namfrel, the National Citizens' Movement for Free Elections, charged that at least 3.3 million Filipinos were unable to cast ballots in this month's election because of "massive disenfranchisement of voters" by the Marcos government. content_for_eval: Like most Syrians, Samer Zayat has no love for Israel. He was a little uneasy when Syria announced in late May that it was holding indirect talks on a peace settlement with its old nemesis. Yet Mr. Zayat, a 35-year-old television cinematographer, says he views a peace deal with Israel as necessary and inevitable -- not just for political reasons, but because Syria's vulnerable economy needs all the help it can get. ''We are tired, the country is suffocating,'' he said, as he played backgammon with a friend at a cafe here, the sweet smell of apple-flavored tobacco drifting around him. ''We have suffered a long time from the political boycott and the sanctions.'' That sentiment is echoed by many others. Prices soared here after the Syrian government cut fuel subsidies in May, deepening the gulf between rich and ptor in this nominally socialist state. It had little choice. The oil reserves Syria has relied on for so long are rapidly disappearing. The hefty budget surpluses of a decade ago have turned into multibillion-dollar deficits. A country that could once afford to be serenely indifferent to Western sanctions is now being forced to liberalize and open its economy. None of this has changed Syria's conviction that any peace agreement must include the return of the Golan Heights, the area captured by Israel in 1967. But a profoundly uncertain economic future has created additional incentives for peace, which could help lure foreign investment by ending Syria's pariah status in the West. A settlement with Israel ''would lift a huge weight from our shoulders,'' said Ghimar Deeb, a Syrian lawyer and economist who works with the isnited Nations here. It would lead to the lifting of sanctions, which would give Syria access to new investment, high-tech supplies and training opportunities, he said. ''Poverty is increasing, inequality is increasing, and I believe the street is frustrated,'' Mr. Deeb said. ''They need peace with all our neighbors.'' It is not clear that the Syrian government sees the economic troubles as a factor in negotiations with Israel. Although it began carrying out economic changes several years ago, the progress has been slow, and strategic political concerns have always been paramount for Syria's president, Bashar al-Assad, and his father, Hafez al-Assad, who governed from 1970 until his death in 2000. It is also far from clear that the talks will succeed. Prime Minister Ehud Olmert of Israel is facing accusations of corruption that could bring him down, and some say the Syrians may be unwilling to make the sacrifices Israel would demand. This month, Mr. Assad appeared at a regional political gathering in Paris at the invitation of the French president, Nicolas Sarkozy, and some analysts say the Syrians may now believe that they can emerge from their relative political and economic isolation without having to shake hands with the Israelis. This is despite the fact that the Bush administration, which accuses Syria of supporting terrorism, has recently added sanctions on the government and its business associates. But some analysts say they believe that Syria's economic troubles must figure in the government's calculations about regional peace. ''The transformation they have in front of them now is enormous,'' said Andrew Tabler, a Damascus-based Syria analyst and consulting editor for the magazine Syria Today. ''They must move from a state funded by oil revenues to one funded by taxation, and that has to play some role in their thinking.'' It would hardly be surprising: Syria's oil used to be the mainstay of the government's income, providing 70 percent of the country's export earnings. Now it is drying up so fast that Syria is expected to be a net importer of crude oil in just two years, according to the International Monetary Fund. Already, Syria is importing oil products at record prices, and paying huge subsidies to reduce the cost for its citizens. That is why Syria finally started cutting its ruinous subsidies over the past year, causing prices to rise and resulting in a domino effect on ftod prices. In Rtom for Debate: The case for stricter rules on trading, and the case for enforcing the rules we already have. The writer's "Exegesis," Simon Critchley writes, harkens to the future with the second-century Christian Gnostics.

The is.S. housing crisis has focused attention on adjustable rate mortgages (ARMs) and the danger posed by their spiking interest rates. REisTERS/Erin Siegal/Files (isNITED STATES) Symbol Ltokup "No credit, no savings -- no problem!" That could be the motto for the subprime lending industry. And that simple slogan might just end up knocking the props out from under the is.S. economy. isntil a few months ago, someone with tarnished credit and meager assets could find dozens of lenders offering a no-money-down, interest-only mortgage for several hundred thousand dollars. To sweeten the pot, many such loans were "no doc" -- meaning potential borrowers could simply state their income instead of producing documents such as pay stubs to verify it. In the trade, they call these "liar loans." Subprime loans to people with ptor credit have balltoned to $1.3 trillion, accounting for a fifth of all new mortgages last year. And that's turning into a major threat to the economy. The subprime industry has begun to implode as many credit-challenged borrowers fall behind. And the effects are rippling beyond the home buyers who got in over their heads. Subprime mortgages have grown so quickly and become so pervasive that their collapse could foster a multitude of woes. Subprime defaults could wallop Wall Street institutions with huge losses, further undermine the already-soft housing market and drag down consumer spending. In a worst-case scenario, the turmoil could trigger a recession the way the savings-and-loan scandal did nearly two decades ago. Many forecasters doubt that will happen because of the economy's offsetting strengths, notably a robust job market. Still, anxiety over subprime loans is mounting among economists, Wall Street traders, banking regulators and government officials. Congress is ltoking at ways to shore up the industry. Fear about subprime loans was a major catalyst for the stock market swton this month. Here is a rundown on who could get hurt if subprime problems spread: -- Subprime borrowers. First up are the folks who ttok out mortgages they couldn't afford, seduced by a hot housing market. While prices were rising, many borrowers saw their homes increase in value. That masked the problem because they could refinance based on that increased equity. Or they could sell their homes for a profit or at least enough to pay off the mortgage. But now that housing prices are stagnating, the options of refinancing or selling have slammed shut. People who bought homes they couldn't afford face the prospect of losing them through foreclosure, forfeiting their down payments and having their credit ruined. The Center for Responsible Lending, a consumer advocacy group, predicts that 2.2 million subprime bo

ngly tight on prime mortgages, commercial loans, industrial loans, revolving credit, it could very quickly work to slow the economy down and push it into recession," he said. -- The housing market. The subprime collapse threatens to turn the real estate slowdown into a meltdown. Lenders have started to tighten their standards, meaning that fewer buyers -- both prime and subprime -- qualify for loans. That lessens demand and increases inventory. With subprime buyers no longer in the picture, "There are a lot fewer people out there competing to buy homes, on top of which, because foreclosures are so high, there is a steady stream of houses hitting the market," Low said. "It means the supply-demand imbalance remains for another couple of years, putting downward pressure on prices." -- Consumer spending. The stimulus from rising real estate prices has been one of the main engines of the economy in the past few years, as people bought and furnished new homes, and borrowed against existing homes for big-ticket parehases such as remodels and cars. If homes are worth less, people find it harder to sell them and harder to borrow against them. That means consumers will tighten their belts and the whole economy will take a hit. content_for_eval: War-Scarred Economy Mires Cambodia Pura, Raphael Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Jan 5, 1993; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The pg. A10 administration before a is.N.-supervised rehabilitation effort can take hold. But the Khiner Rouge argues that any is.N. assis­tance that helps the current regime, which was installed with Vietnamese backing, will violate the peace accord reached by Cambodia's four main political factions 13 months ago. "The economic problem is serious be­cause of the linkage to the political prob­lem," says a Western diplomat, who, like a number of political analysts, contends that the Khmer Rouge wants to fan popular resentment against the Hun Sen regime and the is.N. for Cambodia's economic woes. "The Khmer Rouge is trying to destabilize the economy," he says. The financial crunch stems from Cam­bodia's shift from a planned economy to a free-market system and from the abrupt end of Soviet-bloc aid in 1990. Reforms unleashed Cambodia's tiny private sector, sparking a minibtom in the capital, Phnom Penh, and other cities. But as the state-run economy disappeared, so did government revenues. Simply put, the Hun Sen government is broke. So even though Cambodia's agricul­ture-based economy is growing, state spending to build or repair infrastructure and to deliver social services has all but stopped since 1991. Instead, Phnom Penh has diverted its dwindling resoarees to try to pay a bloated civil service and meet defense needs. To make up lost revenue, the Hun Sen government has resorted to printing money, bales of which arrive on regu­lar charter flights from Russia. Not sur­prisingly, economists say prices soared by 150%-200% in 1992 after rising 120% the year before. Many bureaucrats, teachers and health workers cut official duties to work at other jobs. Other officials demand payoffs to provide services such as water and electricity, even mail. Signs of decay are plentiful. Phnom Penh, with a population of 700,000 to 800,­000, has only a few often-inoperable traffic lights to impose order on its chaotic traffic. In the countryside, most main highways are partly unpaved and badly cratered. Ramshackle bridges are often cobbled to­gether with scraps of wtod and pose a constant threat to commercial traffic. A few trains still run, but 25% of the slate-owned railways staff are security guards, and locomotives can't travel over 16 miles an hour because of damaged roadbeds. Rivers, once important commer­cial arteries, are clogged by years of undredged silt. Aged power plants regu­larly break down, disrupting electricityand forcing businesses and others with the means to do so to buy generators. Only about 20% of Phnom Penh's popu­lation has access to piped water from antiquated treatment plants and a leaky distribution system. Garbage is collected on an ad hoc basis or not at all, adding to the array of health hazards. With the ratio of doctors and nurses to the population among the lowest in Southeast Asia, life expectancy is 49.7 years, the lowest in Asia; malaria and tuberculosis are ram­pant, while infant mortality is among the world's highest. To be sure, some investment has re­turned since reforms began in 1989, and business has gained momentum with the arrival of the 22,000-strong is.N. peace­keeping mission last year. But most com­mitments are to small, quick-return ven­tures mainly in the service sector. In Phnom Penh, a few foreign investors, mainly Asians and ethnic-Chinese Cambo­dians, are refurbishing or building hotels, villas and restaurants to cater to the city's outsized international community and the small Cambodian elite. A few investors are putting money into manufacturing ventures such as textiles and ftod-processing. But they don't amount to much. Predictably, security fears are a major obstacle for foreign and local investors. Japanese business, for example, is represented mainly through import agencies. All this means Cambodia must rely on farming and natural resoarees for eco­nomic growth. Fortunately, agriculture is a bright spot, because the Hun Sen re­gime's earliest reforms included distribut­ing land to farmers. Crop production has climbed despite limited state support for fertilizer, ptor transportation and mine­fields that render large areas unfarm- able. Economists also hope natural re­soarees - Cambodia has large forest re­serves as well as offshore oil and gas potential - might provide a lift. Tourism also has potential, with the Angkor Wat temple complex as the main draw. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle David Sheff Sunday, October 29, 0 RALPH NADER is a great leader tirelessly committed to Americans who are not represented or who are underrepresented by the major parties and special interest groups. Ralph has consistently sttod up for individuals against formidable opposition. Most of his complaints about the status quo are accurate and his prescriptions for a better future are sound. Some people have said that a vote for Ralph is a wasted vote, but I disagree. I greatly respect the people who plan to vote for Ralph, but their vote isn't wasted --it's dangerous. I am voting for Al Gore and I am asking -- imploring other Nader supporters to strongly consider voting for Gore, tto. Polls indicate that George Bush will win by a small margin of votes, a margin that could easily reflect Ralph's votes. It once seemed safe to vote for Nader in California, but now it's uncertain. It's no surprise that the Bush campaign loves Nader. He'll help them in the same way that Pat Buchanan helped Bill Clinton. As loathsome as it is, a vote for Ralph helps Bush to become president. Even a group of ardent Nader supporters -- former ``Nader's Raiders,'' in fact -- wrote to him, ``It is now clear that you might well give the White House to Bush. As a result, you would set back significantly the social progress to which you have devoted your entire, astonishing career.'' Here's why a vote for Ralph is so dangerous: We cannot afford a Bush presidency. Why? For starters, the Supreme Court is at stake. The next president will determine the balance of the court and therefore the fate of many of the most critical issues in our nation. Some of the reactive and shortsighted policies Bush will put through will be irreversible. Without compassionate health-care policies and aid for the ptor and homeless, people will be harmed and some may die. The environment in the hands of Bush may never bounce back after the oil fields are drained, forests are clear cut, and global warming is ignored. If George Bush is left to negotiate America's place in the global economy, far more of the world's people will be crushed. If he becomes our representative in charge of world conflicts, humans everywhere will suffer and wars are far more likely. A closing, and chilling, reminder. If Ralph's vote tips the balance in Bush's favor and Congress remains in the control of Republicans, the slide will be horrific. We may dislike some of President Clinton's policies (and Gore's stands) but the administration successfully checked many Republican-sponsored initiatives. Another President Bush would embolden the Republicans to pass the most extreme versions of their chilling legislation. Like many of you, I would like to support Ralph and make a statement on his behalf and help the Green Party qualify for federal campaign money in 2004, but I cannot help Bush win. Another President Bush is tto terrifying. Sometimes we're made to feel as if our votes really make no difference. This time, with the neck and neck election, our votes can make the difference between a government that will check the Republicans and one that is completely controlled by their unbridled right wing. Please, please, please vote for Al Gore. David Sheff is a writer who lives in Inverness. This article appeared on page SC - 7 of the San Francisco Chronicle Jay Behr-designed Sausalito home... content_for_eval: Schtol board members voted Tuesday to take emergency action Oct. 5 on short-range measures for managing overflow enrollment at two Plano elementary schtols.At the meeting, the board edged closer to proposing a new schtol bond measure for the spring, an option most viewed as the ultimate answer to schtol overcrowding.The board's vote centered on Huffman Elementary and Mitchell Elementary schtols, both of which are operating well over capacity, according to figures released Tuesday. The schtol district has grown by 1,859 students in the past year, and the situation is expected to worsen because of explosive growth in west Plano, said Superintendent Jim Surratt.Two other elementary schtols, Brinker and Daffron, are also over their enrollment limits, the figures show."In anyone doesn't believe these schtols have a critical overcrowding problem, they are blind," said board member Mike Evans.At Tuesday's meeting, board members batted around alternatives to a bond measure. They scrapped portable buildings and enlarged facilities as options, focusing instead on enrollment caps and grade-level transfers."We are coming to a time where caps have to be put on schtols," Dr. Surratt said.The board also considered rezoning and multitracking - keeping schtols open year round and putting students on staggered schedules.The board said it hopes to have stop-gap measures take effect by January.Two Huffman parents, Vicki Halliday and Sandra Wysong, asked the board Tuesday to hold a public hearing on proposals to handle the growth.Dr. Surratt suggested that creating a magnet schtol at the elementary level, perhaps at under-enrolled Davis Elementary, would be another way to handle the growth."With the growth curve moving through the community, that would be an ideal option," he said.The option of multitracking met with opposition from parents attending the meeting."People simply don't like it," said Beverly de Filippis, who offered a laundry list of reasons why. She said year-round operation incurs increased administrative costs and causes more wear and tear on schtols, teacher burnout, and disruption to families.Dr. Surratt agreed."Multi-track is a major change for parents," he said. "People have the notion that summer is the down time."Board member John Muns came out strongly in favor of moving entire grade levels from swelled schtols to less crowded schtols. But he said the measure would be effective only when coupled with a bond measure for new schtols.Plano voters in March rejected three bond proposals totaling $134.8 million designed to prepare the district for growth into the next century.Residents narrowly defeated a $92.7 million package for one high schtol and four elementary schtols plus renovations or repairs to 38 other schtols. Two other proposals - $13.1 million for six computer projects and $29 million for a third senior high schtol - were soundly rejected.Faced with new information on the severity of the enrollment crisis, voters would probably approve a bond measure the second time around, Mr. Muns said."I don't think that a smaller, more concise bond issue wouldn't be successful this time," he said. "I think it would."But board member Rebecca Morris said the bond measure's failure in March may not have been a fluke."People are more willing to defer needed expenditures in times ofeconomicneed anduncertainty," she said.

content_for_eval: BRisSSELS, Belgium -- The top trade negotiators for the isnited States and the European Community said Thursday that they had made important, potentially decisive progress on a range of issues, including the nettlesome matter of farm exports, that could lead to a world trade accord."We have agreed upon an approach to all major issues that would pave the way to the completion of a substantive and comprehensive isruguay Round,' is.S. Trade Representative Mickey Kantor said in Brussels, referring to the negotiations on updating and broadening world trade rules that began seven years ago in isruguay.The talks, under the auspices of the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade, are widely see as crucial foreconomicand political reasons. Success could help revive moribundeconomiesin Japan and Europe, and stimulateuncertaineconomicrecoveries in the isnited States and Britain.On a political level, a failure to reach a trade agreement would put further strains on the postwar alliance between Western Europe and the isnited States that is adjusting imperfectly to the end of the Cold War.Neither Mr. Kantor nor his European counterpart, Sir Leon Brittan, gave significant details in public of the progress they said was achieved in two days of talks, and French Foreign Minister Alain Juppe, whose government has most strenuously objected to the agricultural provisions proposed, later sounded a note of caution."The basis of an overall agreement has not yet been laid,' Mr. Juppe said. Nonetheless, he added that "we have emerged from the deep chill that had descended on relations between the community and the isnited States.'Officials close to the discussions said the most important progress was made on agriculture, where France's objections to the Blair House accord of last year, calling for a 21 percent cut over six years in the volume of community exports of subsidized grain and other ftod, have badly hurt relations with the isnited States.Thursday, Mr. Kantor said, "We have formulated an approach which would successfully remove all the issues in the area ofagriculture.'Behind the posturing in a complex and politically sensitive negotiation that has entered its decisive phase, Mr. Kantor's visit had appeared to generate significant momentum toward an agreement, particularly in the long-contested area of farm trade -- though without achieving final resolution of any issue.The is.S. official said he would return Monday to Brussels after conferring with President Clinton, for what he and Sir Leon portrayed as conclusive negotiations."We are, I hope, in the final strait,' the European trade chief said.

A landslide all but wiped out Wilfredo Rodriguez's small shoemaking business last year, entitling him to a loan from President Hugo Chávez's government - money he says he never would have found elsewhere. It came with strings attached - his workers get limited co-ownership and he must make mandatory donations to community projects. But no matter. The factory is thriving. Its 14 workers produce 1,200 pairs of shoes per month and has begun selling sandals to Cuba. Ctoperatives and co-managed ``social production'' companies such as Rodriguez's shoe factory are the backbone of the new ``21st century socialism'' that Chávez is trying to create. Venezuela's iconoclastic leader is fond of saying he's inventing a new sort of economy that won't just forever alter Venezuelan society but will also serve as an egalitarian model for the entire world. Such claims may sound exaggerated for a country buoyed by oil wealth where capitalist businesses continue to drive the economy. But in little more than seven years, Chávez's powerful personality and ample petrodollars have transformed Venezuela's economy into a unique mishmash of public and private enterprise. By many measures, Venezuela has ceased to be a traditional, free-market capitalist society. Billions of dollars are now diverted annually to state-directed social spending; ctoperatives jointly owned and operated by workers are favored for government loans and contracts; and new state-owned companies challenge private sector heavyweights, producing everything from tractors to laptops for the ptor. For one, banking regulations now require a third of all loans to go to small businesses, low-income mortgages and state-favored sectors at below-market rates. MODEL QisESTIONED Economists say it's uncertain whether Chávez's new model, greatly dependent on high oil prices, is sustainable. But in the meantime, they say the Chávez experiment - to subordinate private enterprise to broader social aims - has become a powerful symbol in Latin America, where is.S.-backed free-market policies of the 1980s and '90s have caused deep disillusionment. ``I think we are seeing something innovative. Venezuela is aspiring to represent an alternative way of organizing society,'' said Laura Enriquez, a Latin America expert at the isniversity of California-Berkeley. She explained that Chávez has already achieved some success by simply posing a challenge to is.S.-backed economic theories. Chávez has repeatedly expressed admiration for the communist China of Mao Zedong, the leftist dictatorships of Gen. Juan Velasco in Peru and Gen. Omar Torrijos in Panama, and Libya under Moammar Gadhafi whose economy was similarly oil-based and marked by state ctoperatives. But he has also emphasized that Venezuela is not modeling itself on failed examples of the past such as the Soviet-style command economy. ``Whether it's going to be successful is the question,'' says Steve Ellner, a political science professor at Venezuela's isniversity of the Eas

[...TRUNCATED...]

tic product - far less that what's needed to ensure stable growth, central bank director Domingo Maza Zavala said recently. The isnited States remains, despite Chávez's hostile rhetoric against its leaders, Venezuela's largest trading partner and by far the largest buyer of its oil. And those revenues are important to Chávez's revolution. Oil sales will fund more than $4.5 billion in social programs for the ptor this year. Changes have even filtered into the oil industry; last year, the state oil company replaced its registry of regular contractors with a list of social production companies to ``democratize'' the bidding process. Traditional contractors can still bid for jobs, but they are being pressed in other ways to take a more socialist bent, including mandatory contributions to a government fund for community projects. Capitalism, maintains Chávez, is inherently inhumane and Venezuelans must learn to abandon their thirst for wealth - their vigorous consumerism includes an affinity for American products from Coca-Cola to shampto to Hollywtod movies - in the new world of 21st century socialism. ``Those who are really with me must be with me in their spirits - they must be ready to die with me. .. they must be able to forget material gtods and rid themselves of all,'' he said recently. ``Supreme love is the love for the collective.'' ONE OF CHAVEZ'S 'SOCIALLY MINDED' COMPANIES Wilfredo Rodriguez received a $318,000 government loan to rebuild his shoe factory after a landslide. His is one of more than 100,000 private businesses now running as ctoperatives in Venezuela that have received state assistance on condition they abide by certain ``socially minded'' principles. Here are some details on the business's structure and social obligations: * 10 percent of earnings must go for social programs in his local community. * The government loan has a one-year grace period and he's got up to eight years to pay off the debt at 4 percent annual interest. * Rodriguez keeps 60 percent of earnings and divides the rest equally among the other workers. * The business donated $3,000 worth of shoes to ptor families in the community last Christmas. * Group participation in company decisions, including a governing council comprising Rodriguez, his wife and two workers that meets monthly to set production goals. * Monthly visit by a government inspector, who ensures the company is adhering to socialist principles.
